content.problemStatement:
- In this video, I'll show you how to **check responsiveness in the browser** using the **Inspect tool**.

- We start with our responsive page already built. On a desktop screen, the layout looks like two columns side by side.

- Now, I right-click anywhere on the page and select **Inspect**. From here, I click the small **Toggle Device Toolbar icon** — it looks like a phone and tablet.

- Once this mode is active, I can test how the page looks on different screen sizes. If I choose a smaller device, like an iPhone, you can see that the two columns automatically stack on top of each other. If I switch to a larger screen, like an iPad or a desktop, the layout shifts back into two columns.

- This is the main workflow developers use to **simulate multiple devices without needing the actual hardware**. It's an easy way to confirm that your media queries and responsive styles are working correctly.

- By resizing the viewport and switching between devices, you can make sure your site adapts well to desktops, tablets, and mobiles — all directly from your browser.

---
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
